costs: Legal drafting and negotiation costs for establishing association-run or network-clustering agreements
Ongoing administrative and accounting capacity for association-run schools and multi-service hubs
Capacity-building grants and technical assistance targeted at low-administrative-capacity municipalities to prevent exclusion of the poorest villages
Teacher salary and secondary-employment support to address commuting/part-time precarity documented in the Polish networked model
content.equity_impact: Retaining schools via networked or association-run models plausibly protects the poorest, most isolated villages from losing their only public institution, but the administrative-capacity burden of running association contracts, multi-service hubs, or inter-municipal networks risks systematically excluding exactly those poorest, least-organized villages that most need protection, unless the state provides dedicated capacity-building grants, model-contract templates, and legal/accounting support targeted at low-capacity municipalities; without such targeted support, this scenario could paradoxically widen the gap between well-organized and poorly-organized rural communities.
expected_benefits: Preserves structural social capital that Polish multi-case evidence shows declines significantly more in villages that lose their school than in villages that retain it.
Retains public funding and curriculum standards under non-governmental management, avoiding a binary choice between full state operation and closure.
Diversifies the school building's function into multi-service community hub roles, potentially increasing fiscal and civic utilization of underused rural infrastructure.
content.goal: Retain village primary schools through inter-municipal pedagogical networks (French RPI-style clustering), community/association-run publicly-subsidized schools (Polish model), or multi-service hubs combining school with other civic functions.

implementation_steps.action: Establish or clarify a legal mechanism analogous to Poland's association-run school amendment, addressing barriers such as changing maintainer law and defining ministerial approval procedures, with realistic allowance for legislative negotiation and stakeholder consultation delays.
Form inter-municipal pedagogical networks or association-run maintainer bodies, with dedicated technical assistance for low-capacity settlements.
Coordinate parallel infrastructure funding channels to support multi-service hub conversion of school buildings.
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Agriculture (Magyar Falu Program) and Ministry/EMMI

mechanisms: Poland introduced a legal framework (gmina agreements with associations, foundations, or natural persons) allowing small schools (up to 70 students) to remain open under non-governmental management while retaining public funding and curriculum.

Structural social capital decline in villages where primary schools closed (2000-2016) was significantly larger than in villages where schools remained open, as measured by a multiple-case study of four Polish villages.

Poland's 2024-2025 draft education law formalizes multi-service community hub functions for shrinking primary schools, including childcare, pre-school provision, and senior services, as an alternative to closure.

Grassroots and civic maintainer models exist as alternatives to closure in Hungary; in Sáta (Borsod), a local community organization took over school maintainership rather than allow the village to lose its school.

Very small rural schools in Poland cannot provide full-time employment to subject specialists, forcing teachers to commute between multiple schools and take secondary employment; approximately one in five rural teachers holds a part-time job, indicating networked models do not automatically resolve staffing precarity.
risks: Association-run and civic-maintainer models risk being perceived as privatization by teacher unions and some political actors, and the state has previously demonstrated willingness to forcibly reverse maintainer status (as in the MET case) when operators fall out of political favor, raising the risk that the same coercive-reversal capacity could be applied to civic/association maintainers.

Organized union resistance may intensify if networked models are seen as a mechanism to reduce state employment guarantees or bypass Act LII (Status Law) protections, requiring explicit political or legal safeguards for teacher employment continuity across network/association transitions.
content.title: Networked retention: clustering, association-run and multi-service hub models
